questions.question: What does the lesson say about support during grief?

options.text: You need one perfect person who understands everything
Professional support is the only type that matters
It is a system of different types of support serving different needs
You should avoid relying on anyone and handle grief independently

questions.explanation: Support during grief is not about having one perfect person who understands everything. It means having access to different types of support that meet different needs — emotional, practical, informational, companionship, and peer support. All of these matter.

questions.question: Why are specific requests more effective than general ones when asking for help?

options.text: Because people do not actually want to help and need to be forced
Because general offers like 'Let me know if you need anything' rarely lead to actual help
Because specific requests make people feel less guilty for offering
Because grief counselors require specific requests before they can help

questions.explanation: People often want to help but do not know how. General offers like 'Let me know if you need anything' are well-intentioned but rarely lead to actual help. Specific requests like 'Could you bring dinner on Thursday?' or 'Can you pick up the kids tomorrow?' are more likely to be met.

questions.question: What is Prolonged Grief Disorder (PGD)?

options.text: Any grief that lasts longer than six months
A normal part of grieving that everyone experiences
A recognized condition where grief remains intensely debilitating well beyond expected timeframes and substantially impairs functioning
A term for grief that includes anger or guilt

questions.explanation: Prolonged Grief Disorder is a condition recognized in the DSM-5-TR where grief remains intensely debilitating well beyond expected timeframes (typically 12+ months after a death) and substantially impairs functioning. It affects a small percentage of bereaved people, and structured treatments exist specifically for it.

questions.question: How does the lesson suggest handling well-meaning people who say hurtful things during grief?

options.text: Cut them out of your life immediately
Accept all comments graciously since they mean well
You can let it go, gently redirect, or set a boundary like 'I am not ready for silver linings'
Respond with anger so they learn not to say those things

questions.explanation: Well-meaning people sometimes say hurtful things during grief, usually from discomfort rather than malice. The lesson suggests you can let it go if you have the energy, gently redirect ('What I really need right now is just someone to listen'), or set a boundary ('I am not ready for silver linings. Can you just sit with me?').

questions.question: What unique benefit do grief support groups provide?

options.text: They provide medical treatment for grief
They force you to grieve on a specific timeline
They guarantee you will not experience grief waves anymore
The experience of being with others who truly understand because they are going through it too

questions.explanation: Grief support groups offer something unique: the experience of being with others who truly understand because they are going through it too. Benefits include reducing isolation, normalizing experiences, learning coping strategies from others in different stages, and having a designated time and place to focus on grief.

title: Support Systems and Asking for Help - Quiz
